And I had to cut out wide open, better like a bull. And then we'd get over there, just after we passed that Candyport outfit and up over the hill there, well there, headlights all up about, somebody ahead. It was just ruts up today, you know, mud roads. And it's nothing but headlights, as far as you can see. And so there's cars stuck up there. Nobody could get by them. Oh, gosh, you know, I looked up on the back fence along here, but there's a bank out there so far from the fence. I said to the gear and I head up on that bank and I bounced for ditches. I went clear and patted a whole bunch, you know, in the rut and in the ditches and up on the banks. It was one of those old cars, I could, and I patted everything. And boy, oh boy, everybody in the country was like, Parpool Billy. One of the Billy's was like, just a damn bull driving in the thing, you know. He was lucky. Yeah, I was lucky, all the boys. Well, I didn't upset the dad. Anyway, we had a lot of fun. Everybody in the car was yelling, you know, hold on there. And then we had 40, 50 cars in that spring lineup there, and I was around a whole bunch and landed out ahead of them and kept on going. And boy, that old Parpool Billy had a 40 horse power motor in it. But had an exhaust pipe about that big around and just better like an old tractor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your parents, did they object at all to your going with such a wild guy?

speaker: Unknown
words: Oh boy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, they weren't too much. But different Harvard people, see I was raised up at Harvard.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, it was like a dog patch. It was like, what do they call them, guys back there, the dog patch or?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was a little Abner.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No, it was probably always fighting back there because.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh yeah, yeah.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, Harvard and Princeton was quite like the country back there, you know. A bunch of those old women would get along the side of the line and they'd look at you and those women. It's like you walked in out of Mars or something. Princeton, I didn't have much use for Harvardites either. So, I went on that way for years. So when I started going around, oh boy, I don't know. And I used to, I could pay my ass for it. I'd do it with that old billy of the ones. And the bell would up to you, you'd go up to the middle of the middle of the truck and you'd leave a dust creek. You'd open the time you'd leave Princeton and you'd get up there and that old car bellaring. And you'd come around the corner and there'd be an old farmer out there next to the fence. Whoa, whoa, whoa, whoa, whoa, whoa.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It's like.

speaker: Unknown
words: Good boy.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Closer. So you know what, take all that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Then when you started going there, they probably thought it was too bad for her.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, I suppose.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: My dad said, I asked him one time, he said, well, he said, I suppose you'll have a better time, you know, have more fun if you go with a guy like him than somebody, you know. I don't even know who he compared him with now.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Probably went and I realized up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Probably some farmer boy. But anyway, he was always good to him. When we had her come by down here, he'd come down and help him harvest and he was, they were real good pals.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, he'd get some soul sacks for me. Yeah, we got along all right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you do a lot of planning before you got married, thinking about, you know. Planning? Yeah, I mean getting married and what you would need to be married as far as, you know, setting up a house and all that stuff. Thinking about the future. Did kids do that in those days?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No, no, I just kind of went from day to day. We just did. Well, we got engaged and she had been teaching, I think, using every job teaching at war yet, I don't think, anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, but I had one more year before I'd have to go back to school and we talked about the meal. And then the meal shut down.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, they had low water and they didn't have enough water to float the log down into the dam. And they had to put the log down and float the log down into the dam. So they had to shut the meal down. So I told Anna, well, why don't we go get married and go on an animal and all that meal shut down. So that's what we did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then we come back and we went to Garfield and got our store and...

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: We rented a house down in Potlatch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We rented a house, well, after a while. Yeah. We couldn't get one right off. It was a few weeks before we got one.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, I don't know. We had more during the house, finally got it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then we moved in with our bedroom set and our kitchen out.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I was trying to have a shoe tray, that kind of thing. Got what we needed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You went to Garfield to get a stove?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, yeah. That's where we bought it. We had a hardware over there. Called the name of Ireland, run us a hardware store. So we went over there and then we bought some other furniture that did be with us. Yeah. Pluse.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then that spring, we bought us... No, you had a .22 and I got me one.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, we used to go squirrel shooting. So I got her a little .22.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just a little one. But man, we were deaf on squirrels for a while. And then I just couldn't see them at all. I just missed every shot.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: She was a dead eye. What do they call her? That woman was a dead eye, what?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, that's enough. But anyway, I could shoot a wire.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: You know where the wire's tabled to her post? Yeah. She could split the wire. Okay. Once she could do it three times.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you use any of that food? Did you use any of that for food when you shot the animals? Or did you use the skins or just for play?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, just to get rid of them. Farmers were almost paying bounty on them to get rid of them. You don't see them much anymore now. They're pretty well done with them. But boy, when I was a kid around here, Jesus Christ, right up this road, it'd be squirrels running back and forth across the road in front of your car all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you get chivalry if you got married? Yeah, we had a shower up in Harvard, and I had one down here to Princeton. And then we had chivalry. And it was just beautiful. Yeah. So many, many, well, two showers, you know.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: We're down all right except one of your old boyfriends had the chivalry. And I just had a box of cigars out there. There wasn't that many people here, but somebody told me afterwards he went out with pockets full of cigars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, he got something out of it anyway.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: He got something out of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I love that that was...

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: By the way, spell chivalry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, I thought there was two ways. The easy way I seen it is S-H-I-V-A-R-E-E, but I've also seen it spelled C-H... Like Shaviary or something. Yeah, that's the way.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: It's French. I think it's spelled the French way. C-H-A-R-I-V-A-R-I. Yeah. Don't sound like chivalry, but...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that pronounced chivalry, too?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, I guess so. You look at my dictionary, Shaviary, and that's the way to spell it, and that's what it is.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the showers like then, Anna? Did they give you gifts? Yeah, gifts. Pillow slips, you know, or blankets, or a lot of dishes. I got that covered back in there. A lot of wedding gifts in there. I'll have to divide up one day.

speaker: Unknown
words: No.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: There's no chivalry in the morning. I don't have any chivalry around here anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did they surprise you? Was that part of it? I mean, did you know the one was coming, or what?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Just for us. Wait till you get to bed, you know, then make you get up and dress. I know one time I got in there, and I got shot by the time of this, Shaviary and my sister. Got married, and we was living on the other place up there. I was sleeping upstairs, you know. So, I think guys, when they started to come in, they were shooting shotguns off and beating on cans, you know, and we had an old shepherd dog. And I never seen a dog as brave as he was, but he went out and met the whole bunch of them. They was banging away on them and raising heck, you know, and he went out there and he... My teacher's school over here printing him Alfred Chabot. I guess he bit him. I mean, anyway, they come on up to the house and...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's... what kind of war is that?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: That's what they call a carnival.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, it's lovely.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Carnival glass, I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then I got two or three like this with sized handels on them. Those are shower gifts? Yeah. Oh, really nice. And then the old stacks of glasses, you know, and fruit bowls and little sausages and, you know, that kind of stuff.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I wonder why they call that carnival glass. Is that what they used to give them, prizes and carnivals?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, it looks too nice for that. Maybe it's the colors because it has the... it reflects so many different colors in it. It has lots of colors in it. And then...

speaker: Unknown
words: Kerry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Reynolds has one almost exactly like this. Only it's tiny and it has ruffles in it.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Oh, it does, ma'am.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It looks like the same color and everything.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, that's carnival glass. But I want to do Prussian glass. At least we have advertising. We've got a problem with the Prussian glass.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were telling me a story about the dog bit...

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Oh, yeah, I bit that guy. But anyway, they come up on the house while all the rocket woke me up, see. I was just a little kid. And so I went and looked out the window. I think it was summertime and I think they had the window raised up so far. And I went and stuck my head out. Because I stuck my head out, I seen a ball of fire right down below me. And so some guy just stuck and pulled his shotgun off, you know, in the air. And he didn't have it straight up. So the next day I went out the house right up just above the window. Where the AAs are on the roof, you know. Bird shot all around up there. The old house fell down now. It was still there. That old shot didn't miss my hand. I got very fired. He just stopped to put the house. He just banged, you know, just like that. And I had my head stuck out there. I was just looking down there. Boy, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when did you first start working in the mill? That was before you two were married, right? Oh, yeah.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I started working down in the mill during World War I.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That early?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: By 1918. That was early after the United States got into it. And I don't think all the kids was able to work, could get a job, because they pulled so many men out, you know. They'd go to war. They'd put boys and women to work. And that's when I started to work, pick an agent down in the mill. I think I was about 17, 18.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: See, if that, now. If that, because weren't you born in 1903? Yeah. You must have been even younger. Let me see. If it was 18 and you was born in three, you would have been 15.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: When did you not take me into war?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think we got into 17. 17th. So you must have been about 14, 15.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No, I wasn't that young. There's something wrong with my story there. It must have been right after the World War. It seemed like I was 17, 18 years old, honey.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they still have a 10-hour day when you first started working there?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No, no, eight hours. Was it eight? I think about 1914, it lasted a 10-hour day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the working conditions like when you went into the mill? I guess the Wobblies had already cleaned it up as far as the 10-hour day goes.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, the Wobblies had already done their good work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But what was the work like? Did you get many breaks during the day or anything like that?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No, the work wasn't too hard. They always had a lot of men. That's what they tried to double the work up on guys like they do now. Now they just, kids go down there and pick an edge and you just sit there and ask them to pick the edges off as they go by off the boards. And it wasn't no hard work. The pay wasn't so hot. The three-dotted boys had a day. A lot of money for a kid that didn't have any money, though. No, it wasn't six days a week. And, you know, when they first started to mill it down here, they started up two shifts, 12-hour day. Yep, 12-hour day. Kept the old mill going day and night. I don't know that run. I started up about 1906, I think, when they got the mills tied right in there someplace. I think it was about 1914, didn't they? Well, they finally got down to 10 hours and then finally down to eight. I think about 1940 would have lasted a 10-hour day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when I've been talking to people in Potlatch, I've heard quite a bit about all the foreigners that worked there. Oh, yeah. Did you know or see much of the Greeks and Italians and Japanese in there?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, I seen that mill, but, well, that was during the... Let me see, that'd be World War I. World War II was... There wasn't over six men that saw me there, and it was white. That was all Greeks.

speaker: Unknown
words: Japs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Japs.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: They had Japs and Italians and Greeks. Well, they had four carriages down there, you know, and they had two men on a carriage. A long time, I was only one of those. One of the whopper Greeks, all the rest of them. But then during depression, that's when they cut them thin that lot. A lot of them took off during depression and never come back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they stick to themselves pretty much? Yeah. The Greeks or the Italians?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, they were. Oh, that guy, they used to be in the mill a separate time, you know. They'd all get around a bunch, them Greeks. And there was Alec McDonald and I, and there was another... There was only about three or four guys in there outside of the showrooms that wasn't Greek. And the guys, they'd get to yakking like something about chickens over there. And Alec McDonald would say, hey, you son of a bitch. I just kept talking. And they'd go back to Greece. You used to give them a hard time. Oh, yeah, they'd congregate by themselves. They didn't associate. Well, I guess you couldn't boil them. But anyway... No, they were only out. One Greek left in Montlage, I guess. I know of. Gostemus? Yeah, old Gus. I guess he was the only one left. I tagged him down there. That's true. Well, I guess old Gus was the last one to bunch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the foreigners were sort of discriminated against so far as getting the better jobs? Do you think they had a harder time than the Americans?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I think they were favored. Did you? Yeah. Well, there was a reason for that. I don't know. I can't prove what I say there, but they'd do anything for a job. And ordinary guys out here, none of them had a marriage. They were all single-bucked, and they'd all back together, and it didn't cost them much to live. But they'd kick back for the boss, see? That's what I've heard now. I've never seen that, but then I think there must be something to it. Because they'd kick back so much a month for the boss, and the bosses would work them up in the job. So they didn't have much trouble working up and getting the better jobs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder how come the Greeks were in the sawmill and the Italians were in the yards. I mean, why they worked out that way. Did you ever think about figuring out why that was?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, I guess it wasn't very, very hard. The Italians in the sawmill, but there was a lot of them out in the planer, and they worked on this. That's when they had those railroads running all over the yard out there. They had a lot of them working out there on the sections and building tracks. And then there's some, just quite a few of them, worked on the green chain. But I don't know why that was. The Greeks and the Italians didn't mix very good. Well, I guess they don't to this day, do they? It's a kind of...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't know. They're both Southern Europeans, but that doesn't mean much. That doesn't mean you... I don't really know. Did you have any friends with any of the Greeks in the sawmill? Oh, yeah.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: After World War II, there was old Gus, John Gano.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: John Gano was a good friend of his.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, him and I were good friends. Gus, I knew him pretty good, but I didn't know him because I did John. But then there was John's brother, Tony. Yeah, they were all good guys.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, they were.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I got along with them all right. But the rest of them, they just disappeared. I didn't know where they went to. One of them, I don't know. Like I say, he squeezed his money. And when the depression come, his name was... I don't know what his name was. They called him Sleepy Jim. That's all I ever named by him, Sleepy Jim. And he was kind of like a guy. He walked with a... you know, like he was all disjointed. But anyway, he saved his money. So I guess when the depression come, he pulled out, and some of the other Greeks told me about him. And you see him and another guy went together, and they bought a big hotel down in Seattle. And then they made money on that, and my guys then bought another one. And I guess they got to be millionaires, both of them, two Greeks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the Japanese? They were pretty much separate from everybody else, too, weren't they? Who, the Japanese?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Oh, yeah, they had their own boarding house. Company built a boarding house just before they japped down there. Part of that. Yeah, they kept themselves pretty well. Well, there's one little chap, used a janitor in a store, and down at the main office. Wapenabe, I think, is his name something like that? He was just a little fella. He had a little wife, and two or three little kids. But then when Pearl Harbor hit, I guess he just railroaded them out to his concentration camp, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, I heard that there was talk... that he was an agent of some kind, and maybe that's what they thought about all the Japanese then. I don't know. Yeah.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, anyway, this guy, he was just a janitor, but yet he had a really expensive camera, and he'd talk different guys to take him around the country, you know. They'd go to Lewiston, and he'd take him pictures. And nobody thought nothing about it, you know, but till after the door went, and they wondered, why the heck, how he could afford such a camera he has. Well, that was really some camera. And then, of course, Pearl Harbor hit, why they figured he was just a work of a spy and suddenly taking pictures of the town of... Oh, that male guy here from Lewiston, I suppose, Lewiston, and dams or wherever they were, and they think he'd get around. He'd take guys for taking him. You know, man, just working for that kind of job. Them days didn't make that kind of money. So, anyway, everybody's just going along with it until Pearl Harbor broke Lewiston, and then all of a sudden, he was ready to take him out and skin him alive. He was a good little guy up until that point. But I got over there, and he disappeared out. I was reading the other day, but a lot of them was good citizens. They'd take them and just, like, call them off like cattle and trucks, you know, and send them in them concentration camps. And it wasn't fair.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But didn't a lot of them know about Pearl Harbor before it happened and get out of here?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, yeah, they started before Pearl Harbor. They started this. A lot of them started disappearing out of the potlice. I don't know where they went. Oh, there was one little Jap Edgeman down there. I worked with him. And, oh, way back in the early 20s, way back in the early 20s. He kept saying, some day Jap angles with this country. I'd say, oh, they couldn't lick one side of it. Oh, yes, they will. Some day Jap angles with this country. My guys didn't know how to do it. They was planning that for a long time before Pearl Harbor. They used to say, some day Jap angles with this country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Maybe he could just see that it was coming because of the history of the thing. I mean, they got a different way of looking at history over there in the east and the Orient than we do here.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, I think the country must have been planning, but, you know, just waiting until they got ready to go. And probably that might have been 10 to 15 years ahead of Pearl Harbor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: See, I would have thought that those foreign groups, now the idea I've got from Gus Demas, I talked to him some, is that these Greeks and Italians didn't speak much English and neither did the Japanese. I think that would be pretty hard on groups like that to be a part of the community if they couldn't speak an English language.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, most of them tried to speak a little bit. John Guino and Tony, he had to be used to them to understand them. But they got so they could talk pretty good. But anyway, they... No, they wasn't bad guys, but most of them wasn't even citizens. That's all here. I know Gus was telling me one time he went back to Greece. Oh, six years ago, seven years ago, maybe more than that time goes. But anyway, he took an extra pair of shoes with him, you know, and his cousin was over there. He used to be here at the mill, but he went back to Greece and got married. So Gus was over there. And by Gus, the people's support, you know, they just almost bought over there. Actually, a pair of shoes was over there. So finally he gives a pair of shoes to his cousin and Gus didn't pay a lot. I guess he'd give a lot of money. Mother, he was pretty well healed one time. She saved his money all his life. Well, I guess he'd give a lot of his money away over there. He kept enough of his figures, lived the rest of his life over here. He'd give a lot of his clothes away, clothes over there. People just so hard up over there, he just couldn't say no to them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He mentioned to me that his sister wasn't in very good shape over there. I wouldn't be surprised if he didn't help her out quite a bit.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Oh, I imagine, yeah. But he didn't like it over there. He went over there to stay, I guess, after he was there. He bought him a ticket back to the USA.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when those guys, the Greeks, I heard the Greeks did quite a bit of gambling and the Italians did quite a bit of bootlegging in here, bootlegging wine anyway.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, the Greeks was as bad as the Degas. It used to be an old Deagle Mike they called him right on away. Mike Petrogalla, that's his name. He was a bootlegger way back. You probably heard him say, when you buy a model of these boards, you can have any color you want. Well, it was black. Well, that was the truth. That was the only color. An old Mike, everyone around, he'd get a new board. But before he'd take it on the road, he'd take it in, Peter Yellow. Peter Yellow, before he'd ever take it out on the road. He'd always tell the old Deagle Mike Kyle, he got down the road. It was a real yellow. I don't know why, but he'd take it Peter Yellow before they ever went on their own.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Listen, do you think that there was much, well, I've heard about that tricks and jokes were a big thing in the mill. It was a way, you know, way past in the time, I guess. You remember there being very much of that when you were in the mill, jobbing other guys, and playing practical jokes. One guy told me that he remembers a favorite one, was sticking grease in a guy's glove.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Oh, I don't know about that. I think it was kind of fun one night down there. I discovered the fire, but I was riding number one carriage. It was up on the carriage. You could look out the window and they had a conveyor run out there. It runs out to the fire hole. Carriage was fired, and there was a fire bolt. I looked down there and I seen a fire burning. So I had a yellow out of me, and I jumped off the carriage, and I had a fire hose up there. And so they had to run like this at the edge of the mill here. They started, the fire hose was here. When they turned it on, it was rolled up on the rear gear. But you had to run it down here and then poke it out the window, down on the fire. So I was going to be the engineer to turn the water on. I don't know George. George Turner. He was one of them, but they went down there, you know that drug that thing that I didn't hold around in the hall or turn around. Well, to turn around easy, I just give it a whine. Coach had a little hose just straightened out. Some guys went backwards over the tracks or in the carriage. Time goes down. And they always go, I've done that a purpose. Well, they always turn around, you know. But being all right, they've been holding it straight. They had that bit out the window. Of course, that old pressure hit that hose. He just straightened out and took them guys with him. They always blame me for doing that a purpose.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You didn't do that on purpose.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Hard to turn around, so I turned around.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How would a fire like that get going?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I don't know how to turn a fire. Could be friction. A good bearing got hollowed like chain or something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the fire hole? You said they got a fire hole there.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, a fire hole. Yeah. That's where they got the fires to heat make the steam.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, I mean the, yeah. Boiler rooms. Yeah, the boiler rooms.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, down there where they watch the fires and boilers up above. Down there where they have to keep sawdust and the fire down there, all them boilers. I don't know why they called the fire hole. They always called it. You know, I'm guys, I didn't do the purpose. They run the hose down there and hoped I would have turned around. So turned around I did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you going down there from Princeton every day to work in the mill in those days? When you first worked there? Well. In the early years you worked there?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: First tired out, another kid and I, we stayed at the boarding house down at Potlatch. Then we come home on weekends. And we both had bicycles. And we got tired of that. The boarding house grubbed in. I haven't played that on yet, so well. So, no, a long time I lived up here on the old place and working night shift. And I'd ride my bike to the mill and back every day. Nights, no headlights on it. I think I did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You motorcycle. What? A motorcycle, not a bicycle.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No bicycle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Bicycle, no.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I didn't get the motorcycle, like, oh, 23 years old I guess, before I got a motorbike. No, I wasn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a real difference, a difference between the local kids who came from the farming country around here, let's say, or the backwoods country and the people who were town people that lived in Potlatch?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, a lot of friction. Town people, always, farm kids, you know, backwoods. Yeah, there was a lot of friction between the town people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yes, it was awful hard down on Potlatch going to high school. You know, the town kids just about run that country kid out, did one, or have a friend that they used to, she had to ride a horse to school way out in the country, you know, in order to come to school. And they went like that, holding those, because they said she smelled like a horse. Smelled like a horse. She took it until she couldn't take it, and then she couldn't. But I had a better deal than they did, because I had to work in Potlatch for my board, and then I would go to school. And so after a while, I considered a town kid.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Where did you live with the better people? Well... How much more?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, I don't know if they were better people, but they sure thought they were.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I thought they were.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you were going to high school, you were working for your board at the same time? You were working at the house you lived at?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, you know, you worked with the people you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They'd take it. And the thing you'd do then was to have your little servant girl, and you'd go in and get a family to live with, and they'd feed you for doing the ironing and the dishwashing and taking care of the kids and stuff like that in the evenings and mornings so they could go to their parties and whatnots. I worked one year and a half for the store manager down there, and then I worked another year for...

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: That's as much as he made. He had down in the basement of that old pool. It was his idea. He really didn't benefit that old ice cream. Ice cream on the stick and then truckers around the audience. But he didn't know exactly the formula. He had the experiment, so I helped him down there. And of course, every stick wouldn't work where I could eat it. I put the powder on ice cream.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He must have...

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: The way they do it, you know, they...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That sounds like a pretty good job.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, it was no good for nothing. I don't know. We worked down there for the biggest ever night, trying to get the temperature just right so you get that ice cream just cold, you know, and you dip it in this hot chocolate and pull it out, and I'd leave a coating of chocolate out. But if you didn't have the chocolate hot enough, our ice cream wasn't cold enough. It just wouldn't work. So he had his thermometer there. He'd get that chocolate just boiling hot, you know, and he'd get that ice cream on a stick and shove it down that chocolate and blow it up and get everything just right while you could just take them and just shove them in and just make them just, you know, put them to it, you know. I don't know how many chocolates he'd shove down. That didn't work. He didn't have a chocolate hot enough, but the ice cream wasn't cold enough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he just sell them at his place or did he sell them around the country, other places?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: When he got them, he tried to sell them right down to his own joint down there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, then he shipped them out to other towns.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, he got the patent on them, I think, and then they went out. But I don't know. And I went to Portland, I went in the joint down there in Portland, you know, and asked for an ice cream at Slope High and they looked at me like I had horns on. They didn't know what they were talking about. But anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Anna, was that what an, did a number of kids do that, come into live in Potlatch to go to high school? Or were you special? Oh no. Did you get that? No, no. Anybody that could come that could stand it, you know, and make any difference how they got there. When the year I graduated, there was only 14 or 15? Mm-hmm. 15 kids, I guess, graduated that year. 15 kids from Potlatch and? And all the country around. But I mean, did the kids from Harvard, did they, if you wanted to go to high school in Potlatch, let's say, would you pretty much have to live in the town if you came from so far away as Harvard? Some kids came in every day that far.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: One thing about Potlatch, they run the school, they run everything. They hired a teacher, it's a Potlatch limber company. They hired teachers and they built the schoolhouse, they kept the schoolhouse up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But they let you go without paying. Yeah. And the district we come from had to pay. And they did something for us.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Oh, then for high school?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But probably the family didn't pay. No, no, the family didn't. But did your family think it would be easier for you to go to school by living in Potlatch? Was that the idea? Yes, because they did, because we had no way of getting there. And they had to go on to school. And so that was one way of doing it. Well, I had been working in Potlatch anyway in the summertime, you know, and so I wasn't new at that job. And then when I went to high school, why?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: That's about the only way a country could go to school, you know. They pretty much had to go to town and live, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If you lived two or three miles, you know, you could ride a horse. But like living up at Harvard, I'd have to go down there and stay.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: When they left, moved to the family, finally moved over to Princeton, and then she'd walk to school back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, and your family moved to Princeton? Yeah, my family moved down to Princeton, and so we started walking. Most of the time we walked the track, railroad track. Do you think it was hard on you when you were a kid to be working in the welded and people's houses and to go to school? I mean, did you feel that was a hardship then? Yes, it was harder working for my board lot than it was walking. Because, well, you had so much work to do, you know, for the family in order to earn your keep.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, they'd treat kids, you know, like that. Not all families, but some of them, it was like the white man used to do the Negroes, you know. He used to serve it. And that's all he was, he used to serve it. How was it? That aunt of mine done you when she was, she'd made a dentist, I apologize. For a pay, she'd give you some old dress or something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, she said that if I'd come and work for her, well, she'd give me a daughter a week, you know, besides my board. So I changed. I had Miss Spain in a good place, but a person needs money too. So I went to work for her. And I don't think I ever collected the daughter. She would give me some old block she'd worn out, you know, and she'd say, well, you take this for your pay, and let's see, it's about worth $6. And so that'd be six weeks I'd work for that thing. Probably I would never have it on. But, you know, that's the way it was. And I was glad to walk. Kids, I think kids can be used pretty easily that way, you know. Of course, I knew what work was. My folks had a big family, and I was the oldest girl, so I knew what it was to take care of kids. But the thing I didn't like most of all was earning. And I did. I had ironed every night, but in over...

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Did they have electric irons at that time?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I suppose I don't know. Maybe, I don't know, was it electric, or did she just heat it up on a... No, it must have been electric. But she had two little kids, and she addressed them in the morning, and then at noon she would change their clothes, and they were ready for... tell their daddy to come home. So that was two cents of clothes every day, besides his white shirts that ran down to the store. And I just... I was so weary every time I'd get ready for bed. And I got up in the morning, got the kids up, and gave them their breakfast.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Prillin almost threw their board, and I bet you didn't eat enough to...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I'm not there, I didn't.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Oh boy. No, that was cruel days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But at Elsie's I got more than I wanted, more than enough.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, that's what I was saying. It depends on who you work for.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think these people who were living on Nob Hill, I've heard about them, that quite a few of them lived above their means, really.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Oh boy. They all kind of a funny thing in doing that. During the war, World War II, there was a family living up there in Nob Hill, and he was one of the poor men down at the mill. And they were living high. But anyway, down at the garage there at Potlatch, a man named was a tableman who was running the garage, but all of a sudden the government pulled it out, I guess, that anybody didn't pay that money bill, cash bill, you know. But for that month, well, you couldn't get no more credit. Well, I didn't know nothing about it, but it just happened I made a little money and I went in and paid my bill up there. And so that law went into effect. And so I know I came up to the garage to get some gas, because I had to drive, and it lives on the upper place up here, driving back and forth to work. And this high-gluten lady, she drove in there and told her to fill her tank. And so, I guess she drove in. So they wanted her money, and she said, charge it. And he went back and looked on you. They had pages and pages of names there, you know. If your name was on that list, well, you didn't get no gas. So anyway, I'm looking over and she says, you'll have to pay for it, because she didn't have the money to pay for it. So they drained the gas out of her tank. And, of course, I... Yeah, they just drained the gas back out. That's all they could do. The law said they had to have their money. And so I pulled right behind her, and finally the gas hurt her, shoved out of there. I pulled up and told her to fill her up. And the guy looked at me and went back in, and I went in with him. I guess I shouldn't have been, but he was going down and list the names, you know. And I didn't have my name on it, because, like I said, I just had to cut them. He was lucky. I wanted to pay my bill up. And so I guess my name was not. I acted like it almost hurt his feelings. He was so sure, you know, he was going to tell me to go, you know. It just got out of there. But I guess my name wasn't on the list, and I believe it really hurt his feelings, but I got you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, those people that you worked for that didn't care how much you had to eat, Billy, they just used you like a servant pretty much. Yes, they just, no matter, you know, you had it to do. But the first people I stayed with, I was invited to the table to eat, you know, right with the family. But the second time, then I wasn't, I had to eat in the kitchen. I had to serve them and go back in the kitchen and eat what I could. That's the time. And I don't know. I don't, now that I'm old, I don't care much for them.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: You know what?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't care too much for them. But she was pretty good to brag on me. Well, my girl does this and my girl does that.

speaker: Unknown
words: My girl.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But I got in trouble more times there because...

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Who's that?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Walter's.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: What Walter's is?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He run the store down there and he would bring, you know, candy and stuff home. But, you know, I knew better than to touch it. But I had company. My girlfriend from up at Harvard stopped in to see me. And she saw candy there and she saw candy. She just, hey, you know, and she eat and eat and eat. I didn't know how to stop her. I was too dumb to say, well, that's not for me. And holy cow, next morning did I get it. They just don't sound like nice people as far as...

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No, they wouldn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It seems like they should have understood that you were as good as they were, even if you were working for them. Yeah.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, it was a whole lot like the attitude I turned about the old manager at L.E.A.D. I put off the road down here. He was so important as Hicks. I was supposed to get off the road for him until I taught him a lesson. Then he found out my guy said I wasn't paying off. But then that was the idea. He said that. You couldn't say he fired the chauffeur when he got in trouble. But yet you couldn't say he was riding down the street and everybody would get off the road so he would go through. And he wasn't enjoying that. He's enjoying that. Watching all the Hicks get off the road for him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But it was nice to be riding down, you know. It was nice to have the ladies riding on you. She bragged on you to other ladies? To her friends? Yeah. You know, well, my girl does this and that. I told my girl to do this and that, you know.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: But they had a two-class society. Now they'd have dancers down there at old gymnasium. And just to dance with her, big shots. Well, I heard, but we couldn't go there, you know. We were outcast. And of course we'd go in and get up on the balcony and look down and watch the High Bucketbox dance dancers, you know. Anyway, I don't know. Some guy got a whole bunch of thick bombs someplace. I was up there. I didn't know it. But I guess you'd throw them down the dance floor if you don't. How did they long to break up the dance? And over there, he was so furious. Oh, if he could have found out who'd done that. How bad? He never found out. But that was the last dance I had. That was that. High Bucketbox dance. That was the last time they ever had a dance. That broke it up. No more of that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did most of the houses on Knob Hill have servants, have a girl working for them? Was that the common way to do it? No, I didn't think they would. Anybody that could get you to work for them for nothing, you know. And if they could keep you, well, that was a big thing. But then I stayed with old Dr. Thompson's and Mrs. Thompson wasn't a jewel. She was off of pipe. She'd be at the table and she'd look all over. Do you want a slice of bread? No, she said, what do you want? If I said bread or something like that. Whole slice or half a slice? Of course, I'd always say half a slice. But that, if I'd have said a whole slice, she would then...

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Give it to you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She was generous and all that, but she wasn't one to waste anything. As soon as I understood that, I just... And she was very careful to see that I got enough sleep. She was the one that taught me the most about how to get along with those kind of people. She was one of them herself. Yes, she was one. And she was a gracious lady or whatever you call it. She truly was. And then her kids were bigger than your general babies too. And so they didn't need any babysitting with them. The kids would ask, can I do this and that or the other thing? I'd have to decide whether they could or they couldn't if their mother had... If they hadn't asked her ahead of time. But that was a real nice place to stay. She taught you how to get along. Is that like... Were there really certain things you were supposed to do or not supposed to do when you were dealing with those people? Yes. And she told me, for instance, one thing, it doesn't mean anything, but it's one thing I remember especially. She... I always wash my hands before I go to do anything. Well, this time I had this soap in my hand and I was just really washing. And she says, well, dear, I would just take a little soap and then wash my hands with water. I just wouldn't wash the soap. Well, that's really what I was doing. And so that was one way she taught me. And just little suggestions in that way with anything. When you were living with the store people, were you supposed to not speak to them until you were spoken to them, that kind of thing? Or did they have rules like that? I don't really know how people are supposed to be when they're supposed to be waiting on other people. Well, I don't know. You just...

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: You kind of ran around the corner and you spoke to them, wasn't you?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, and you don't interrupt any conversation or just keep scares.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: And kind of an irony that happened, you know. Like I say, my aunt, she made a dentist. And of course I had to work for her. And by the guys you know, she would just load me out, you know, just nobody, you know, just a servant. Well, it turned out I started to go in there and marry her, see. Well, I had not only worked for her, but she knew a lot of the going on around the house, you know. I got my eyes closed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That woman would scream to the top of her voice, to scream and scream and scream. But then long, long afterward I saw her and Potlatch had advanced down there, and she had gone down to change her shoes, and they had to do a more comfortable pair of shoes. And she said, how are you, Anna? She said, I am extremely glad you got into the family.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: She was a big guy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm pretty happy about it too, except for you, Wady. But she knew.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, that got her a goat, and I got married by a guy, you know. She knew Anna knew going along around that house with her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But to get up in the morning, you know, she'd leave me a note here, leave me a note there, and if I'd miss a note wholly, things would erupt. One time she left a note on the kitchen stove. I guess it was a kettle-setting on it or something. Anyway, I looked around and I didn't see the note. And what she wanted me to do was put a boiler on the stove and fill it full of water so the laundry maid could come in later and do the washing. And I didn't see that, and I didn't put the water on the stove, of course. And I just did what I had to do otherwise. And pretty soon the laundry woman come and no water on the stove, no hot water to start with. Oh, man, Pearl was mad, because she was wanting to get that woman out of there, you know, before noon. But if she'd like her notes in the same place so I could find them, sometimes you could go in the living room, and if she happened to be there the night before when she thought of what she wanted me to do, she'd write the note and leave it there. Just inconsiderate.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: She wasn't a rich girl herself. She just raised on her farm down here, you know. You know, families had a habit of doing that. Maybe they had a half a dozen kids or more, but they'd pick one kid out, and they'd lavish everything. Well, it happened to be that family. It was the older kids. They had to work and do without and everything else. But the last two of them, I worked with my granddad down there in the hay, and you know, when my uncle, he was about three years older than I was. But his mother wouldn't let him work on that. When I was in work behavior, it wasn't good enough work for him. I could go out there and work all my life. But he couldn't know. Most he could do was take the car to the town, get closer to nothing to haul out. And my aunt, his sister, she just had a round of pretty. My mother would go down and help my mother in the kitchen getting dinner ready for the men, you know, cooking. And my daughter, she sat out in front of a porch by a guy, she was looking pretty. She couldn't get her hands in the soil. That's the way they raised her, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She is a great, bloody daughter than ever was. Well, she got herself a dentist, but she was pretty well to do. Yes, he was. And that shot her into the upper crust. She never let anybody forget it. But then they, they, one more day, they did a divorce. She was suing for divorce.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: When he died.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he died. And then, then, she married somebody that I forgot who. You know?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: You've been around probably a lot more than I have, but you know, I like how they worked on construction in Portland. You know, a real midget there, a real rich man. Her, his wife, don't make a difference. You know, you'd never know. I didn't, I'm just a real downer working down there. But I was working on a man named Bowles. He'd made his money in World War I in the shipyards down there on the coast. Bowles. And he'd build a big, well, it was just a three hundred thousand dollar house up on the Council Crass, that's important. And I worked on that job there. And he'd come around, you know, his old clausky hat on, standing around. I thought he'd just side walk above him, you know. He'd stop you, you know, ask you about something, talk to you, maybe talk to you two years. I wondered why he was talking to him when the boss didn't come around, you know. He'd keep holding you up and talking to you. He was supposed to be working. I couldn't figure that out. The boss didn't come around and tell me, they'd all have that talking business to get to work. Later I found out I was talking to this guy who's built a house, you know. And his wife, the only thing is, they'd never drive the same car. He had one of them old Franklin cars. They were cool, called Fraglins. And he had one, it wasn't a new one, it was an old one. His daughter, when she'd come around, she'd drive her past this car. She'd drive her out and she was pretty enough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it's a difference between the old rich and the new rich, or the ones that are trying to get rich, maybe?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, just two bit millionaires is the worst kind of millionaires there is, two bit ones. You know, somebody gets a few dollars more than the other guy and everybody's supposed to sleep them, flute them, and they go down the road or something. Now, I always call them two bit millionaires. But you take a real millionaire, a rich man, and my guess, you know, you can be around him and then he's, you know, they don't allow the guy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They don't brag. They can talk with a common man without kind of putting down, you know, or he just...

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I got it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't know if it's still that way, but maybe, but probably in the old days it was.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, it's not that old. Today, it's not so bad, I don't think, because it used to be. But I got a friend, I went to another kid that was running around here at home. His father died when he was small and he had to quit school and work for money to make the, you know, how his mother and his sister lived. They just didn't have nothing. They just, nobody, poor, is what he was in his post. And anyway, he got a whole little bicycle someplace and the front wheel went out. And I don't know, my mother sat in the front of me working at the spokes and stepped to build a new wheel. And I helped to build that wheel up. And anyway, they were just so poor and pitiful. But he got tired out and he made a good mind, he was going to sell back and finish. Well, he was a back-winded boy and he couldn't talk, you know, strangers. He was the last guy in the world used to go door-to-door to sell back and finish. Well, he said it was nothing ever harder in the world. He put in the first day, so I was back and finish. And the second day he said he just might give up with some woman letting him in and bought a back and clean up of it. But otherwise, they'd just go door-to-door to see him and back him in his land with door to his place, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was he selling? Locally, around here?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Pougand. That's where he started out, Pougand. And, but finally he made a sale. So that guy, she's all gung-ho after that, you know. He really wears after them. And today he's still in back and cleaning business. Now that was when he was a kid. When he first started out, he started up in Pougand. Now he's head office is in Portland. And maybe you've seen the papers talking back and cleaners.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He's got stores all over the country, though. He owns it now? Now it's his company? Yeah.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, he's retired now. But he's still got the, he's still owns it. He's a general manager of all of his stores. He's retired, but the company he owns perches him with Cadillac every year, brand-new Cadillac every year. I guess they do yet. I don't know what they was up to a couple of years ago. Every year he gets a brand-new Cadillac to drive, and he has his credit cards on the company and anything he wanted to buy. And then he has a salary so much a year from the company. But he's a millionaire time and again. All the presents, selling that for his back and cleaners.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then during the war, everybody was selling everything they get to get little money, and he went around collecting back and cleaners.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, he'd done that before the war. He's seen ahead.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: First war or second war? Second war.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: First war. No, wait a minute. That was the depression. Depression. Depression. The first time. He went ahead and he had a shop down there. Oh, it was a big place. Shelves. He just had them shelves. Every place you could pack a second-hand back cleaner on them shelves.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Spoke hands still?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No, that was in Portland.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But he's got a store in Spokane, yes.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, he still got a store in Portland, but his headquarters was Portland land. But he was seen somewhere there. Well, he's in the back and cleaning business. Before World War I, before the depression, everything was boom, boom, boom. People were buying cars, buying this. They'd trade in the back and cleaners. Well, he'd take the old back and cleaners and sell them a new one. And then if he'd go out and buy up some guy up there, sell them back and cleaners, maybe he'd have a dozen there. He'd go and make a deal for them and get them. Well, anyway, how they'd run me around and get them back and cleaners? Well, then the depression hit. In course, nobody could buy a new back and cleaner. So he hired a guy and they went in and he picked up a shop and he'd remodel them. He'd get new brushes and repairs the motors on them. And then he'd paint the handles. Then he'd put that paper wrapper around the handle and it looked just like a new one, you know. Well, he'd probably get the darn thing for a dollar and a half. During the depression, he'd probably sell it for $6. It'd be second hand, but it looked like a new one probably. It was all battered up before he was pretty slick, you know. He'd look like a brand new machine. And then come in and he sold all them off. But nobody else could tell anything. People would buy a vacuum cleaner for him second hand. Well, that was a big go and then he got another wrap out during World War II. Doing the same thing. But anyway, he braced out. He's got a storage in Eugene, Seattle, Tacoma, Bratz Pass.

speaker: Unknown
words: I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When this guy was young, did you have the feeling that he had that kind of go get it kind of...

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No, he didn't. He didn't. You just couldn't imagine him going ahead and doing anything like that. If he was going to judge all the kids in this neighborhood, he's the least possible to get their place in the world. It turned out just the other way. He's the only one that really went to places.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder, you know, there's this... They used to talk about rags to riches. You remember that? I read about that that was something that was a big idea. Anybody could become a millionaire. I never very much believed that myself. It seems like so terribly few people do get rich. Do you think that from a case like this, it means that anybody can get rich or you have to be extremely lucky?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, nowadays, of course, some days, you know, there were so few rich people around the United States. There were so few of them, really rich people. But nowadays, there's so many people that, you know, they can hand their wealth down to the kids, you know. And then at this time, he was a regular rags to riches. He does all on his own. He didn't...in the United States or in nothing. He was just...and today, well, he's, I was going to say, in that company now, and he was up here a couple of years ago. I guess a couple of years ago he was up here last. Anyway, we went fishing. Him and I went fishing up at some point. And, you know, he's driving a big brand-new Cadillac. That didn't make no difference. He'd go along, you know, in old cars and passengers. He had his automatic speed control on it. If he wanted to drive a 50, he'd set that. And he'd just set that up and talk, you know, single-pills and downhills. That's all he had to do, he'd set those tears. And he had another thing that come out on us. It flopped. It was that business of headlights. You know, if you had that magic eye in it, if you met a car coming, it's dim. You'd see them having...that had that on. Well, we were coming down to the standpoint, that old road used to be pretty crooked. A lot of curves, nothing downs. And that magic eye business. But another thing that happened with that magic eye... Well, we went over the hills, like this at one place. And there was a car coming. Every time the car comes over the hill, there's lights and dim. It's probably a mile away. And then the lights would come on brighten and go down and come up. But there's always dim, just after that guy... They'd hit him right in the eyes before they dim. But they'd dim, and then they'd turn back. The danger probably was, you'd come down here and go around this curve. They had them black markers along them guard rails. And them darn things would dim the lights. Right when you knew the lights were going around the curve, they'd step out here in the bridge.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Pretty bad idea, right?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, it wasn't a good idea.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But it seems like you take most of the guys that work in the mill, I mean, those guys don't get rich. They can work as hard, harder than anything.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Then by a guy's, you know, they'd lay you off. There was no time, except for like he was 65, you know, like pretty tired now. But back there, it was just like a little horse. They'd just turn you out the pasture, you know. You didn't have no pension, no nothing, no company. So you said, well, this is it. You don't need to come back anymore. We don't need you. Well, a lot of them didn't have any more than just... They'd rent their houses from the potlacks down there. They'd rent their houses. And I don't suppose half of them had $50 in the bank. There was no money laid up, you know. They just couldn't do it. Well, they just went on the county. They just had to go on the county, county aid.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think that in the old days, oh, in the 20s and in there, when people were working at the mill, you think they thought that they had a pretty good chance of really getting ahead good, or were most people just trying to...

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: They just tried to eat three meals a day. They just knew that it was, you know, it's $3.40 an hour for common laborers. And like I say, everything, if you had a car, gasoline was around anywhere from 28 to 35 cents a gallon way back in them days. It wasn't cheap. Cheapest I ever seen in gas was five gallons per dollar. That was 20 cents a gallon. That's the cheapest I ever got. Five gallons per dollar.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, it seems like these days, people... A lot of people seem to have hope that they're really going to get ahead, really going to get a big, you know, a big stake. Was it the same in those days? Were they going to really get ahead?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, some people can say more than others, but... You see, it's funny though, when I got these little things over, you take some guy from one or two kids, and by a guess, when he gets to every time of the age, he won't have... Maybe the guy who had six or seven kids, he'll have a home paid for it. It don't make sense. I mean, they can work the same way, just with the guy with six or seven kids, but I guess when he gets old where he can't work, he's got a home paid for it, but the guy by a guy's making maybe one or two kids, when they get up old, I just don't dreamt of him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, right now, now, it's awfully hard to feed a family and get ahead. It seems like to me, I hear more young people, you know, complain about, you know, not payday, you know. I don't know what I'm going to have for supper. I'm just, you know... I eat up everything I got, and I just don't know what we're...

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Of course, people live so much different nowadays, you know. People don't do a lot of car, they have to take meals off, feed off your table, and they still got a car. Poor people just didn't have cars, you know, they'd walk to work, you know, walk back, and maybe have an old horse to drive to town, you had to get something heavy in the pack on your back, while they drive a horse to town, poor people just... It wasn't everybody that just had cars back in them days. And I guess I did have cars, like I say, a gasoline went up there 28, 30 cents a gallon, you know, and you just couldn't afford to drive your car to the pot right in the back. Heck, I walked even when I had my own car. I walked to work in the back, and I guess all week, there's a car set in its head just to save gas. And a lot of guys up here, Princeton, walked to the mail every day and walked back, night and shift, and they went to the different ones and said, let's walk back and forth to work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sometimes they caught the train, too, it was only a charge of ten cents, didn't it, to ride the potlatch?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Fifteen cents. Not bad. I worked a lot of times, I'd go over here on the night shift, I'd go over here and catch that, what they called the three o'clock train, and go down through Princeton. And I'd get on it and ride the potlatch for fifteen cents, and then I'd walk through home the night after work. Well, those whole gang was almost like Cockney's Army coming up the track, you know. I wasn't all alone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think the guys that lived out in the country and worked in the mill in potlatch felt a little more independent than the men that lived in town, or wasn't there any difference?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, they usually had a little more they could claim their own. Most of the guys in potlatch, they was trying to live a little bit above their means, I'd say, because they'd try to have better furniture, and they was always in debt. Well, just tastes like when the Depression hit. They put me, a lot of guys, I was lucky, I got a job night watching. I guess you'd call it lucky, in a way. I had to work twelve hours a night, but then they'd do my stuff. I'd work half the week, and another guy'd take the ship, and the other half, and you'd buy that work up between us. But I was out there in the yard at Potlatch, and every evening, you could see a car pickup going down the road of the car, but I had new cars. They'd haul them out of town, repossessing them. When they got through hauling cars out, I bet they were one of a dozen cars left in the whole town of Potlatch. I'm not kidding you. I'm not kidding you. But I didn't go for that new car business that time. Then I got married, she had a little Ford Roadster, a twenty-seven model, and I had a Ford, a twenty-seven model coupe. Well, she'd use her car to go schooling back with us. I had that little Ford, so when I got married, we didn't need two cars. We decided a twenty-seven Dodge sedan come up for sale down there. It was a pretty good car, so we decided to trade a two-port job and get that. So that's what we did. So when the depression hit, why, the old Dodge was all paid for. I can tell you though, I went like them other guys, you know, and laid a salesman around trying to sell you a new car. They were working on it, the person had all the more, they were working on it, they sold a lot of cars. But gee whiz, when they got through all the cars out of town, I tell you, there wasn't very many left.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder why they were like that in Potlatch. People couldn't own their homes, right? It was all company. I wonder if that was partly it. If you couldn't own your house, you wanted to own something, so you got a car.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, it was a thing to do, you could get a car. It was clear that little bit of a past distinction yet, you know. I wanted to try to have a little better car than the other guy, you know, bigger. And that was still going on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think that doesn't operate so much out in the country here or out of town.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No, if you're not out in the country, it didn't seem to make much difference what you drove. I don't know. It didn't seem to make much difference. But in town, that way, why? I don't know. They said it was across the hall to see the other guy, what he had. Anyway, it caught them all as a pattern down there, I guess, when the depression hit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Quite a few of the guys out here, families here, had a little bit of land, too, didn't they?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: A little stuff had a difference. A lot of them lived over here in Preston, you know. And a lot of them was farmers working down there, you know. One of my guys, they'd lay off in a harvest and put their crop in, and then they'd go down and work all winter down to the mill, you know. And they made pretty good. They'd say, yeah, yeah. But it was done all right. It was pitiful the way it was. There was nothing to look ahead to, you know. You just went one month a night, and if you could keep your nose above water, when you got old and didn't need you, it was a hit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It really was truly hard. I know one month you was paid $13, your check was, and our gas bill was $12. Depression?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Depression, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So that wasn't a very good bill. But he had insurance, and we dipped into that. That's what put us through the Depression.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, a lot of guys, they just got laid off, period. Well, they could go down to the store, you know, and hold their hand behind their back and do the check and meet, and they could get the grocery bill, you know. And I was out there walking, running around the snow in the wintertime, making $27.5 an hour. That's what they paid during Depression. $27.5 an hour. And, well, I was doing all right, but we had a house right up there. We didn't have too much for our insurance. And we just got married, you know, a year before.

speaker: Unknown
words: A couple of years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really, two years and a half before it really hit us hard.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, by the way, yeah, we got married in 29, I think, because they shut the mail down in that last part, so it was 32. Anyway, but they had a system there that it was working. Now, we only had two light bulbs in the house, and we didn't have the electric stove or nothing. But my bill, my gospel electric bill was going up, you know, eight, nine dollars a month, you know. Well, they were using me to pay my neighbor's bills, you see. They were pouring it on to the guy that was working to cover the other guys. Well, that's more than I could take, so. The house that my dad had up here on the hill was big, and so I told our dad, he was just pulled out of town. So we moved up there. I don't think that was a mistake.

speaker: Unknown
words: Why?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I don't like to have anybody shoved onto me like that, you know, but...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was it a mistake? Because you had all the money?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, it just worked on hardship all the way around, and... I could have just paid down potlatch, you know, and I guess paid up eight, nine, or whatever they wanted to try to make, because I had electricity. But then that was it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It wasn't your electricity, though. No. We only had to help pay for others.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: So anyway, I told them to take the house and said, we moved out. Well, we stayed out there, I guess, a couple years, a couple of letters.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We stayed there. We moved up there in November, and then we stayed that winter, and another winter, and then we moved out sometime.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Next spring.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think it was in the fall of the year when we moved back to potlatch, wasn't it?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, they started the mill up again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how... What was it like when you were living up there, you weren't working at the mill? Yeah. You were still watchmen?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Still working, yeah. I watched a while, and I worked in a plane a while, Lee.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Three days a week. It sounds like that was really going back to land, then.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah. Yeah, it was like, oh, we done all right. But I never did have to take a hand out during that depression. Well, I'll say that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But he, like I said, he had that insurance, and he cashed it in, you know. And then we were careful, living up there, no rent, no lights, no water. Well, I mean, we paid for him.

speaker: Unknown
words: But...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then we milked cows, and his folks brought their herd up there, and it fed them out for the winter. And so we had a cow to milk, to get our milk and butter. You see, that was a big help that way.

speaker: Unknown
words: Yeah.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: It was hard. So anyway, we was tired up in 34, and we moved back to Palach. And I stayed there until about 43, I guess. 44, I quit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, and your dad died in February, and we moved up here then to take care of your mother, and that was 43.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, I worked a while after that, though.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, you worked till 44. Yeah. When you lived there, back up on the hill, did you stay at home most of the time?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah. Yeah, she wasn't in school then.

speaker: Unknown
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it really a whole different kind of a life to make things stretch during the Depression? As compared to before? Yeah, well, we truly weren't too bad off, were we, Emmett?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No, we wasn't. There's just a meltdown, kid, Alec McDonald, who we worked on the carriage together. So anyway, well, we were up against her now, so I had that old dodge. And we went over here to Kendrick, and the guy up there raised beans. Well, I got two sacks, a sack of white and big hundred pound, 140 pound bags, you know, sacks of beans. How was that? He got two.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You got two and he got three, wasn't it, Emmett?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I guess I was good. Then we were a little duff in the back of that old dodge, and I was home. You'd get them for about a cent of pounds, you know, beans at that time. And so my guy said the whole joke was, when beans I hauled on had to take care of lives that winter, he said, Pop, I should have died of death without having his beans. And the bad guys was more truthful than the bad guys.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And up there I had a garden that you don't have in town. Were you giving your beans away?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, we didn't try nothing for them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You had a garden in group and grew most all the vegetables? Yeah, after the first winter. And we got a chicken house built, and we had chickens. And it was... Did people get together very much during the Depression? In the neighborhood? No, you couldn't. Do what? Get together in the neighborhood. I mean, neighborhood get-togethers.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Not too much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Not then.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Everybody stood very close to the fire. Oh, they had summertime. They'd eat one half one another out in Hagan or something like that, you know. Farm workers would help one another out. What I had, the other guy didn't have, or my dad hadn't, and they didn't have a lot of swapping done between the farmers. Couldn't nobody have any money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We worked hard up there. We worked in the mill and then helped with the hay and, too, you know, and different things like that. I cooked for the hay crew, so it was a busy life, you know. No time to waste. Had orchard up there.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: When was it I bought the patent place?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, it was after we moved down from the upper place. Yeah. And back to Potlatch.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, they had 40 acres up there named Earl Patent.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he...

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: He had all pretty well cleaned up, you know, and had a nice garden bottom. Probably got buildings on it. But his dad passed away back in Minnesota, and he had a farm back there, and he wanted to get rid of this so he could get money enough to go back there. Well, my guy said, you know, the 41 point was $1,000. Well, my guy said, it just wasn't as easy as buying somebody $1,000 in a day. Finally, I talked to the banker, and I raised $1,000, and got that 40 acres. So that was my first land purchase. And then I see... Oh, I steamed around and got a whole little land one way or another. I got 160 acres away. We had a tree near Biola back in the mountains, timber claim. I got that just paying back taxes on it. Of course, I didn't get nothing off of that. I gave it to my boy Frank before I ever seen the darn place. I never could find it. I don't know. We only looked for it, but we just couldn't find the darn place. So after I gave it to Frank, and then he got made with a sibear, and they got a pretty fondant. Got a lot lined where it was. But he turned around and sold it for $9,000.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think so, but I don't know.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I think $9,000 was sold before. I got paid taxes on it, but I didn't get nothing off of it otherwise. I gave it to him, and he sold it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When I said get together, I was thinking of parties and dances too. I know I've heard that they used to do that quite a bit.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: They did as a kid. They used to have house parties. They'd have a dance over this place in one year and one week, and somebody else would have it the next week, during the winter times especially. That's from around the time. One time I was just a kid over here, this guy over here, he was a big log house there. Maybe I told you about this. Slim Callahan.

speaker: Unknown
words: Callahan.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Slim Callahan. He was a long lady, lover Jack, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In the 4Ls?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Something like that. He was a good guy. He was just a lover Jack. He wasn't married or nothing. There was a kid that was dancing in the house. His kid was outdoors horsing around. I wanted to come on him out there in the back of the house down at the Louisiana man's morning and growing. Well, I knew him a little bit because he worked for my granddad, the family down there. I talked to him and he said, I wonder who I was. I told him, you're a good boy. So finally he said, I'm going to give you nothing. He pulled out of his pocket to find out a bill and give it to me. And oh boy, I thought I was all of a sudden millionaire. So my guys, I took it. So I went home that night in the dance room. Told my dad what happened. That guy gave me, oh, I got $5. Holy smokes. He worried I shouldn't have taken it, you know. So anyway, the next day was Sunday. Anyway, they got me up early and they put me on the car use. I took off and I... The lumber camp was way above Howard's someplace. I'd never hardly been up there, you know. I'd been up there a hard part of Harvard, but beyond that I didn't know. Had little coyotes. The name was Dolly. And I started out that morning and I had to go and get that money back to that guy. Like I said, you might as well not come home. That's not what I was meant to do.
