And so I was all day and way late that evening. But finally I found him up there in the logging camp. Of course there were other guys around there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So I...

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I thought I'd find him and I'd give him his $5 back. He didn't know nothing about it, you know. Well, I said you'd give it to me last night and my folks said I had to give it back to you. So he'd think I was kind of embarrassing around the other guys. He'd throw his money away like that with his drug. But anyway, boy, I tell you, no drug man ever gave me money after that. That was the end of that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you guys were going together though in those days, then dances, they didn't have them anymore at that moment?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Oh, yes. No, no, they mostly owned the halls. They had a hall up at Harvard and they had provisions. Well, they had one down at Candyport down here. Big provisions down there at the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They're all gone. The dances, they just don't have them anymore. But would people come from all around to a dance like that? Oh, yes. Would just people from the neighborhood or...?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Some people go and they had a... Some people maybe a bunch of go-to-a-vines up here. They had a helmet. They used to have a provision up there, big round hall dance halls. I've heard about that one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I've heard about that one. That was quite a big place.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, that was a big place. And then they had one down at Candyport down here, ground hall there. They had one over at Troy. He was on the road going out to church Moscow. On the left, they were going out to church Moscow. They had a big round hall there. They had a big round hall. They had a big round hall there. A lot of people just, you know, they go to the distance. The next day they go to the next one. That's the way they'd go. Every Saturday night they'd probably go to a different hall.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I know I've heard a lot about during Prohibition. They used to drink and had quite a few fights outside of those dances. It wasn't unusual anyway. Did you get very much... very much lectures from home about that kind of thing? Anna, did they try to make the young ladies have the fear, the alcohol, liquor, and that kind of thing? Did parents try to be very strict about that? No. No, we never... Well, I never had a drink and a dance in my life.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I don't know. I never did either.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't even think the girls...

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Younger people didn't go for it. Mostly just older people go to dances and raise all the heck.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You as a young sport, you never had drinks at the dances either?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Maybe just a little sip or something. I never got drunk or anything like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: One time I know he took a sip. Somebody invited him out to the car and reached out and pulled out a bottle. But you never got drunk or anything. Oh, no.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I never was that crazy about this stuff.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But... If they seemed like they drank this fight, there's always people that go to the dances to fight.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Get a bottle of Carcitude and they have to fight.

speaker: Unknown
words: And then away.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But it doesn't seem as though we went to all that many dances. For a few years we did, and then... What's the matter? Did we grow up?

speaker: Unknown
words: I don't know.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I was only one time at the A- I don't remember that. Was it all ages of people at those dances?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: All ages. You know, kids dances and then old women around. I didn't get old fast enough. I mean, I missed out on that.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, it just don't happen no more. They don't dance around the country no more. Unless the school kids have a dance in the gymnasium down here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just school dances.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: By the dance hall there is one in Oak Island, I know it. It's just the thing of the past. Televisions, I guess, is a...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Took its place.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I mean, I haven't even got picture shows. I haven't got a show building in Blues, potlatch. You've got to go to Moscow or Lewis and get to the theater.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of those picture shows in the 20s? Did the kids- well, kids think very much of them? Were they really great stuff or did you laugh at them or what?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, you know, kind of a funny thing. It takes on them old flop stories you run on television. You probably wouldn't even turn the television on to watch them. But them days they'd put a show like that on and put it up in lights and advertisers. People would come all over to watch them, and there was nothing to them, you know. But the same thing on television. People would give it time of day to turn the thing on for it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then there was those wild West, you know, and they were really, well, not for crowd, we'd go there on the West.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Oh, Wild Bill, and... Bill Hart and all those. Oh, what was that? One of the first Cowboys on the theater. Bill Hart. Bill Hart, he's one of the first ones. Oh, one of his shows, come on. But the thing is, except people of Wild, there's... Oh, they come out Spokane with a big show like... Oh, that old Civil War show. You know, I can't think of names. It was a hooper day.

speaker: Unknown
words: But...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: O'Hara... Oh, so it's Gone with the Wind?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Gone with the Wind, that's the one. Well, they come out. Well, when they come in Spokane, the whole neighborhood just practically moved up to Spokane that night to see that show, you know. Go to the Spokane, see, kind of like yourself. Our mosque, or Lewiston, where was that happen to be? Well, they had a cycle like that for a while. People didn't go for these little ding-dong shows they had around here too much. Just maybe people, young people, just go because there's nothing else to do. But anyway, I'll tell you, I think some of the new shows come out. The tribes and neighborhoods just moved to Spokane or Lewiston, wherever the show was. And they'd run that thing there in Spokane, maybe for three weeks, you know, before the playhouse.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I heard what somebody told me that there were some local girls who would think they wanted to go to Hollywood and become movie stars. That was one thing the movies did when they first came out.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Oh, yeah, same thing. That was a big thing, getting into the movies.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you know, I was thinking, Anna, when you're talking about working in Potlatch, it seems really tough. I mean, it's too bad. If all you were getting was your room and board, you know, for doing all that work, I mean, you must have worked hours every day for that. It started early in, and some nights it would go late. And in the meantime, you had to get the kids to bed and tell the little guys, see how it was nice, stories, you know, to make them sleep. And I had one girl, I imagine she was five, and you'd tell her her story, and she promised to lay down and go to sleep and all that kind of stuff. And you'd get through telling her the story you had to get through pretty soon, you know, because you had work downstairs to do. And she would start screaming, and she would cry and cry and cry. Finally, one of the neighbors told the woman I was staying with, I must be beating that child to make her cry that way. And so she jumped onto me, and I told her that what the little kid would do, you know, and you know, she would believe that. But then the next time I went up there and she was a screamer, I spat with her little, you know, sit down real hard, you know. And I flopped her down on the bed, and then I turned off the light, and that was a no-no. And I left her in the dark a little while. But I went up and changed it pretty quick, because I thought maybe your mother would come home and not see a light in that room. And she didn't. So I didn't end up, but then there was some ladies, we just went through the year and doing fine. Wasn't there, there wasn't any other way that a girl could live in Potlatch without having to, you know, I mean, you just about had to work out if you were going to live there. Mm-hmm. And putting her, everyone on Mobile and all those around had their girls.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, that was the thing, you know, high society, heavy girls, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did the boys from Harvard also live in Potlatch to go to high school? Well, my brother lived out on a farm just outside of Potlatch.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: He'd done chores for a little.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He went to school three years, and then, I don't know why, whether that farmer died or moved away or what, but then that was as much schooling as he got. He didn't go back the fourth year. He did chores. Was that real hard on him, or was that not as bad as the kids?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, it depends on who you were, but it wasn't that hard.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't think it was so bad. You see, he didn't have any little kids to deal with, and he would just do his chores, pick apples or whatever was had to be done, and he liked it. Did you have any other girls, friends that you knew who were working in Potlatch too, like you were? Yeah, the girls got so, they treated me like a town girl, and I really didn't mind high school if I had time enough to study with them. Yeah, no time. I mean, were there other working girls that you knew? Were most of them country girls too, or were some of them town girls who were working? Were some of those other girls that were working on Nob Hill, were they town girls, or were they country girls too?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: All country girls.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: All country girls.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Girls lived in towns. They had their old homes. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get very lonely and homesick being in there? Well, at first, I did. But then, I got over it, I guess. I must have. Because in my third year, some awful things. At your Harvard home? Yeah, at home. The family at home got sick, and my dad came down and asked me to come home and help. Well, if I did that, then that would be the end of my schooling. And so, I just told him that I'd sure like to finish school. I just didn't want to go home, and that'd be the end of it. But you know, I don't know if I'm over that yet or not. I regret it. Because...

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: They all lived.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Mama's, yeah. They were all real sick. But they'd been sick so many, many times. Flus and pneumonia and different things, you know. We had to pull them through. And John had, what's that one? Disease? Fever?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Rheumatic. Rheumatic fever?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, no. You'd get out of water, see him. Anyway, he had that. He was sick months and months of that. He got it at Antwaud, you know.

speaker: Unknown
words: Typhoid?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Typhoid. I couldn't think of that word. Your father let you stay, then, because you asked him if he would. And he understood it, you know. And then he... I went ahead and finished in school, and then I went down to Lewiston. He helped me through as much as he could. And he was... it was good, hard and awful. But one time I wrote up to him for some money, and he didn't have that much money. So he wrote me the nicest letter, tell me I'll send him what I can, and tomorrow I'll send the rest. That was when I was in training. And the girls each had to pay something toward their board in training. So I was no easy.

speaker: Unknown
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Then when I started teaching, I started giving them back the money that they...

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: One funny thing, you know. She was boarding down there when she was going to college at normal. And a bunch of girls lived in a great big two-story house. Each one, some couple, three out of the room here and room there, you know. I didn't know what the old house was. It had a lot of rooms, I guess. But anyway, the snow was so deep. Right down in Lewiston, after I went down to Lewiston, it must have been a good place to snow on the ground. And that's when they really wanted to come down and take them up to a dance. So I found a place where they lived, and I'd never been there before, but... I went in and there was nobody in the bottom of my wazing room there, you know. And so I was standing around there waiting, just to see if anybody was like in choir. Pretty quick, a younger girl, I guess one of the other girls' sisters, younger sisters. She'd come and gallop them down the stairs, and she'd see me standing there, and she stopped and looked at me. And I said, I'm in Alleglies and live here. Oh yes, follow me. Well, she took that back up the stairs again. Well, I guess that was no no-man's land up there, you know. But anyway, she said, follow me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you did.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: And so she come up there and she went back to the door there, and she said that. And so she's rubbed the door wide open, and her and her girlfriend, it was in the room, they wasn't expecting company. But anyway, I went back downtown to get the old car that didn't have no heaters in them, you know. So the good was an awful cold night and the wind blowing blizzard. So I went down and got some heavy men's socks, you know, the big wool socks. I got a couple pairs of them, but the girls were on their feet going home. But anyway, I went down and got them and come back. And of course, I went right back up there. I'd been up there once, but I didn't get killed, so I went back up. And all the other doors were along the hallway there, you know. You'd pull the girls, and you could see the doors. You'd crack that much and a bunch of eyeballs up and down there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So we'd pull those socks on and left, and that girl kept those socks.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Didn't get them back to me. She didn't get me, but she got my socks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it pretty nice living in Lewiston? Did you enjoy that? Yeah, I thought it was. We just had one room, and the bed was in that room, and quite a big bed, you know. There wasn't much room in the table, little round table. The stove was out in the hall. It wasn't convenient. But I think we got it for $15 in it.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: A room, huh?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I paid $7.50, and she paid $7.00.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I imagine about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were most of the girls living there going to Lewiston normal?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, they were all two kids, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In two or three rooms, there were young girls that were going to high school. Did the house have some kind of a matron or somebody living in there to look after everybody?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, they had the landlord.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And if we had a boyfriend come to meet us, or, you know, he was supposed to stay down into the living room downstairs. It was fixed up for the men. But that girl didn't tell him that. She said, well, she said,

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: come down there and she wouldn't expect anybody. She just come and galloping down the stairs, and all. Why don't you see me stand there, and she looked, you know. I said, well, we're going to Lewiston living here. Oh, yes, yes, come on. Up the stairs she went.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you embarrassed by that either, or did you? I didn't know how to get him out of there right quick before the landlord caught us.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Anyway, she doesn't even knock on the door, you know. She just about turned and showed the door, and there she is. I stood there with my mouth open. So anyway, I never had a come forward, so I said, well, I'll go back down there and get some wool socks for you girls to wear home. So I went on out, and boy, everyone them daughters was on their eyeballs up and down, girls in all their rooms.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But nobody told on us, I guess. I never got in any trouble over there.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: They never got in trouble.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, when you talked about during the Depression here, and it being pretty hard, but getting through, how was it with the other families living around neighbors? Did they have it as hard or harder, or not so hard as you guys did?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Oh, everybody was short of money. Maybe one or two guys around the country here, bigger farmers, might have had a little money, but then the ordinary everybody was just hard up for money by a guy's. Everybody had a charge account to store it, but that's what they lived on, and lived on that charge account in Hope. They didn't have money to pay a lot of it. But that kind of finally wound up, and things got to going again. Now we're in a mess, and I also made people out of work if it wasn't for handouts, because we didn't get them handouts some days like we do now, you know. I guess you didn't have unemployment insurance and food stamps, and things they got nowadays, you know. People think they're bad off, but they're really not bad off compared to what it was during the old 20-90 Depression. Actually, they don't know what Depression is for people nowadays. They have to pinch in and change their ways a little bit, but people actually buy guys 100.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: People around here?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I only know one girl, I guess she just must have time to dance. She wasn't much of a guy anyway, but the neighbor girl up here, she married a guy, and he got a cat in way back up in Newwood someplace. That's where he was living. Well then he'd leave her there, and take off, you know, and go to Moscow, and horse around, and buy a guy's jacket, and she just stopped to try to dance. She would go home, her parents lived up here, but her parents weren't agreeable, they were marrying that guy in the first place. If she'd have pride, she wouldn't go home. So I guess time they found her, but I guess she's too far gone to say she died anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she die? From malnutrition probably.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Malnutrition, I think that's all she did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: During a lot of deer killed, you know, during that Depression. Some people even went to extremes, you know, to get a lot more than they used.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, they just, the game warden just didn't see nothing. You know, you go out and go there, but the way it is though, when you'd have turned people loose, that would give them that privilege. It was nothing to walk up through the woods and just see it dead there like that. That's the hindquarters taking off and the rest of it was laying there. And even during that time when meat was hard to come by, you know. So it don't make no difference what you do. There's always somebody that'll make them muggies out of it. Always somebody's like us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did people around here like Roosevelt a lot when he got into here and lost support? Oh boy. Oh boy.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, they sure did. They sure did. In the first term and the second term. The third term, I guess, was he was in four times, wasn't he?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, but he didn't live long.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No, he didn't live long before. I guess it was the third term when we got into World War II. Well, then getting in that wall was a little bit mixed, a little bit mixed feeling. He was still a big hill. He was a big hill to people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I heard something. Somebody told me that in the 28 election that Laird came down to the mill and got all the guys together at lunchtime and got up and gave a speech right before the election and told him that if Al Smith got elected president the mill was going to shut down and that was going to be the end of it.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, I don't remember that part, but the bosses was noisier than that around it. If Al Smith got elected, no. Yeah, old Hoover was running against me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's 28, yeah.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, old Hoover was running against me. The Democrats got in. Let me see who was president before Wilson.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, that's back.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: That goes back behind.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, Wilson was in there in 1914.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, we are a Democrat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: 1912. Yeah, Wilson was forgot to be president in 1912. Before him it was Taft.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Oh, Coolidge. Coolidge was after Wilson.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Coolidge was after.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: After, yeah. He was a president all during what they called the boom days of the 20s. Well, that's what they called the boom days. Everybody was making little money, spending.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that true? Was that true around here? Was it boom days? Was it boom days here?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Oh, yeah. That's what I was telling you about pot life down there. People buying cars like crazy, you know. Everybody was running around in new cars. Anybody didn't have a new car. It was just nobody. And then they went in all little cars. They had big cars, you know, kind of boards you'd call them. And by gosh and all, that's where they got caught. Well, then by gosh. I'm getting ahead of myself. I'm back in depression again. Was that what we was? Due to depression.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were you talking about, about Coolidge in the 20s?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, I was in the 20s. Then in the 28th, Coolidge choose not to run. And so, Hoover then was a guy. Well, everything was still going good that time yet, you know. Everybody was happy. When Hoover got in, and that's when he ran against Al Smith.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, you were saying, you were saying, you were saying there's something about how the bosses were against Smith.

speaker: Unknown
words: Well...

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: The biggest blacker that was up to the last line I remember of was when Roosevelt ran against Dewey. When he ran against Dewey. That was a big thing then, that noise around at that time. I know if Republicans didn't get in, that middle shut down, you know. Well, Dewey didn't get in. Old Truman got in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And so...

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: That was the one I remember of. But you know what happened? They started to make two shifts. They had to run one shift before, after the election was over with. I was done if I didn't belong to my shift.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm going to have to get going, you know. I noticed there, is that right? Is that after 4.30?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, that's before 23 to 5.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, Laura gets to Moscow about quarter five, and she's going to wonder where I am. Well, she'll know if she knows I'm up here, but I should really get going, so... I won't keep her waiting too long. But we're...
